Total NFT market capitalization, top ten projects by market capitalization

NFT total market capitalization, top ten projects by market capitalization

Data Source: Coinmarketcap

Top NFTs in 24 Hours

Top 10 NFTs by Sales Growth in 24 Hours Data Source: NFTGO

Headlines

Pump.fun Announces the Establishment of Investment Department Pump Fund

Pump.fun Announces the Establishment of Investment Department Pump Pump Fund will foster the development of startups within its ecosystem by establishing long-term partnerships with projects. Pump Fund's first initiative is the launch of a $3 million BiP Hackathon: investing $250,000 in each of 12 projects at a $10 million valuation, and providing selected projects with support including mentorship from the Pump.fun founding team. NFT Hot Topics 1. Animoca Brands Co-founder Yat Siu: NFTs Are Not Dead, Wealthy Collectors Still Support the Market Animoca Brands co-founder and executive chairman Yat Siu stated that although the NFT market has cooled significantly from its 2021/2022 peak, it is not "dead," and the current market is primarily driven by wealthy digital art collectors. He pointed out that monthly NFT transaction volume has fallen from its peak of over $1 billion to approximately $300 million, but this is still a significant drop from zero five years ago. Yat Siu emphasized that many high-net-worth buyers are "holding rather than reselling," forming a community culture similar to that of collectors of Picasso art, luxury cars, or luxury watches. While his own NFT portfolio has declined by about 80%, he has never intended to trade it in the short term, viewing it as a long-term asset. Regarding the cancellation of NFT Paris, the flagship French event, Yat Siu believes this is not a problem inherent to the NFT industry itself, but rather a reflection of France and Europe's overall shift towards a more anti-crypto regulatory stance. This, coupled with recent security incidents targeting crypto professionals and investors, has weakened market confidence and reduced attendance.

        DeFi Hot Topics

        1. Portuguese Regulator Orders Polymarket to Cease Operations in the Country

        According to Jinse Finance, the Portuguese betting regulator SRIJ has ordered the blockchain-based prediction market Polymarket to cease operations in the country within 48 hours, after betting on the presidential election results on the platform exceeded €103 million (approximately $120 million). The regulator pointed out that Polymarket does not hold a Portuguese betting license, and Portuguese law prohibits betting on the outcome of real-world events such as politics, only allowing betting on sports, casino games, and horse racing. Currently, Polymarket is still accessible in Portugal, but the country's regulator may soon require internet service providers to block it.

        ...

          2. Aave's Cumulative Loan Issuance Approaches $1 Trillion

          According to Jinse Finance, official data shows that the cumulative loan issuance of the decentralized lending protocol Aave (from January 2020 to January 2026) has approached $1 trillion.

          3. Hyperlend Announces Upcoming TGE and Launch of HPL Token

          On January 20th, Hyperlend, the lending protocol within the Hyperliquid ecosystem, announced an upcoming TGE and the launch of its token, HPL. Staking and locking features will be available shortly after the TGE concludes.

          The total token supply has not yet been disclosed. The token economics are as follows: 30.14% allocated to ecosystem growth and incentives; 25% for genesis distribution; 22.5% allocated to core contributors; 17.36% allocated to strategic investors; and 5% for liquidity. According to Jinse Finance, Aptos has launched the decentralized exchange Tapp Exchange, aiming to become a core infrastructure layer in the Aptos DeFi ecosystem.

            5.Binance Wallet Meme Rush Now Supports the Flap Protocol

            According to official news, Binance Wallet Meme Rush has announced that it now supports the Flap protocol. Users can discover and trade the new Flap token on Meme Rush through the web and app. Flap is reportedly a one-click social fair issuance and trading platform dedicated to achieving fair and simple token issuance, unleashing the potential of the community, and driving large-scale tokenization adoption.

            Disclaimer: As a blockchain information platform, Jinse Finance publishes articles for informational purposes only and does not constitute actual investment advice. Please establish correct investment concepts and be sure to enhance your risk awareness.

data.title: Is there any chance for Kaito?
data.description:
A recent change to X platform API policy has led to the collapse of the InfoFi ecosystem. If I were the founder of Kaito, what options would I have now? Key Takeaways X's policy change caused the collapse of the InfoFi ecosystem within three days, exposing the structural limitations of relying on centralized platforms. InfoFi projects face five options: shutdown, a bounty-based funding platform, a Korean-style sponsored blog model, multi-platform expansion, or an MCN-style KOL management model. InfoFi 2.0 is likely to evolve into a smaller, more controlled model, shifting from permissionless scaling to collaboration between vetted KOLs and projects. Two fundamental challenges remain: establishing a fair compensation system and proving the value of the token. InfoFi Collapses in Three Days On January 15th, Nikita Bier, the product lead for the X platform, posted a short message stating that applications offering rewards in exchange for posting on the platform would no longer be allowed. For the InfoFi project, this marked its end. According to Kaito founder Yu Hu, the events unfolded as follows: January 13th: Kaito received an email from Platform X indicating a possible review and requesting immediate clarification. January 14th: Platform X sent a formal legal notice, and Kaito submitted a legal response on the same day. January 15th: Nikita Bier's post became public, and Kaito, along with others, learned of the decision. The market reaction was ruthless. The KAITO token price plummeted, and the community criticized the team for failing to provide prior notice, despite their claim of being prepared for the situation. Kaito issued an emergency statement that evening, explaining that it had previously received legal notices from Platform X and resolved the issue through a new agreement, therefore it was awaiting further discussion this time as well. Regardless of the explanation, one decision by Platform X has brought the InfoFi ecosystem to a halt. In just three days, an entire category collapsed, destroyed by a company's judgment that it had damaged the platform's quality. If I were the founder of Kaito, would this mean the end of InfoFi? Projects like Kaito are already preparing for the next step. However, what is needed now is not a continuation of past models, but a different version of InfoFi 2.0. If I were the founder of an InfoFi project like Kaito, realistically, what options do I have today? By examining viable paths forward, we can begin to outline what the next phase of InfoFi might look like. Shutting Down: This is the simplest option – cease operations before funds run out. In reality, many small and medium-sized projects are likely to enter a "zombie" phase, essentially inactive, only occasionally posting on social media before disappearing completely. Since the product-market fit built around Platform X has disappeared, shutting down may be more realistic than burning money to find a new direction. If the project has usable data assets, it can sell them to other companies to recoup some value. Therefore, most small and medium-sized InfoFi projects are likely to choose this path. Bounty-Based Grant Platforms: If API access to Platform X is no longer available, one option is to revert to an older model. KOLs directly apply for events, submit content for manual review, and receive rewards upon approval.

Source: Scribble

Scribble is a representative example. Projects post funded tasks as bounties, KOLs create and submit content for review, and then receive rewards. This is a "submission-review" model, rather than real-time tracking.

This structure can scale as an open platform. The platform provides mediation and infrastructure, while individual projects manage their own activities. As more projects participate, the KOL pool expands. As the KOL base grows, projects gain more choices.

The downside is that it introduces uncertainty to KOLs.

If the submitted content is rejected, the time invested is wasted. After multiple failures, the KOL is likely to leave the platform. Sponsored Blog Model South Korea's sponsored blog model follows a "select first, manage later" approach, rather than a submission-after-approval process. Organizations like Revu have been using this model for over a decade. The process is simple: the project sets a target number of participants and launches the campaign. Applicants register, and the project selects KOLs based on factors such as follower count and past performance. Selected KOLs receive clear guidelines. After content is published, it is reviewed by operations staff. If it does not meet the standards, revisions will be required, and penalties will be imposed if the deadline is missed. In this model, KOLs avoid wasting their time. Once selected, compensation is essentially guaranteed as long as the guidelines are followed. Unlike bounty-based systems, there is no risk of rejection after work is completed. From a project perspective, quality control is easier because only pre-screened participants are selected. Multi-Platform Expansion If Platform X is no longer viable, the next option is to move to YouTube, TikTok, and Instagram. Within Web3, there is already a strong push to expand beyond Platform X. The view is that real growth requires moving away from platforms dominated by crypto-native users and towards channels with a broader audience. The main advantage is access to a much larger potential user base than Platform X. Platforms like TikTok and Instagram are particularly influential in emerging markets such as Southeast Asia and Latin America. Each platform also runs different algorithms, allowing operations to continue even if one channel is restricted. The cost is operational complexity. On platform X, only text-based posts need to be reviewed. On YouTube, content length and production quality matter. On TikTok, the first three seconds determine performance. On Instagram, story execution and format quality must be evaluated. This requires platform-specific expertise or new internal tools. API policies and data collection methods also vary by platform. In effect, it's close to rebuilding from scratch. Policy risks remain. Platforms might suddenly change their rules, like X did. However, distributing activities across multiple platforms reduces reliance on a single platform. For larger projects, this is the only option that provides meaningful scalability. MCN-style KOL Management In the Web2 MCN model, the brand value of KOLs is important. In Web3, this is even more decisive. Narrative drives capital, and opinion leaders wield excessive influence. A single comment can affect token prices. Successful InfoFi projects have cultivated active and aligned KOL communities. These KOLs grow through months of engagement on the platform. Projects can retain this community and shift towards data-driven governance, rather than relying on the continuous discovery of new creators, as traditional Web2 MCNs do, which require finding creators from scratch. An MCN-style structure implies formal contracts, rather than loose, voluntary platform participation. With accumulated data and established relationships, the platform can exert greater influence in the Web3 ecosystem and negotiate better deals. For InfoFi projects, this requires a robust management system. Data becomes the core asset. This model can provide a sustainable competitive advantage if KOLs can be guided through data and the project can be provided with a professional, data-driven GTM (Go to Market) strategy. InfoFi 2.0's collapse left two lessons for the Web3 ecosystem. The irony of decentralization: Web3 projects relied on the centralized X platform, and a single decision by X was enough to crash the system. Limitations of incentive design: Rewards successfully attracted participants, but there was no effective way to control quality. The increase in spam gave X platform a clear reason to intervene.

Source: X(@nikitabier)

Does this mean the end of InfoFi?

Not entirely. A few projects that have found a product-market fit are likely to survive by changing their format. They can move towards multi-platform expansion, curated events, or MCN-style management.

InfoFi 2.0 may be smaller, more controlled, and more quality-focused.

InfoFi 2.0 may be smaller, more controlled, and more quality-oriented.

It will shift from an open, permissionless platform to a censored network, becoming more like an integrated marketing platform that combines local GTM efforts with components such as offline advertising. However, fundamental problems remain. Joel Mun of Tiger Research House points out that once rewards are introduced, participants inevitably seek ways to exploit system vulnerabilities, making it difficult to design a fair structure. This behavior leads to low-quality content and creates a negative feedback loop that can undermine the platform, a critical issue for the InfoFi project. David raises an even more fundamental question. He argues that the value of the InfoFi token is supported less by platform performance and more by staking airdrops and a belief in the narrative. Now, both have lost their relevance. This raises a direct question: why would investors buy InfoFi tokens? These questions need clear answers for InfoFi 2.0 to survive. A project cannot be sustainable if it becomes disconnected from its token holders.

data.title: Taxes on overseas income retroactively paid up to 2017? How can taxpayers resolve tax crises?
data.description:
News Overview

On January 15, 2026, according to multiple media reports, the retroactive period for tax payments on overseas income of mainland Chinese tax residents has been extended compared to before, now extending as far back as 2020 or even 2017. Since 2025, many tax residents have received notices and reminders from tax authorities, requiring them to conduct self-examinations of their personal income both domestically and internationally and to promptly file tax returns. The retroactive tax payment period mainly covers the past three years, primarily 2022 and 2023.

On January 16, relevant departments of the State Taxation Administration of China stated that tax authorities will continue to strengthen publicity and guidance on tax payment for overseas income of resident individuals, and since last year have reminded taxpayers to conduct self-examinations of income obtained from overseas between 2022 and 2024.

FinTax Brief Review 1. Event Interpretation: Tax Payments for Overseas Income Retroactively Valid to 2017 1.1 Event Content and Background Recently, a report from a well-known media outlet regarding "retroactive tax payments for overseas income" went viral on various financial platforms, sparking heated discussions. The report pointed out that the statute of limitations for retroactive tax payments on overseas income for mainland Chinese tax residents has been extended compared to before, potentially extending as far back as 2020 or even 2017. The day after the aforementioned report was published, relevant departments of the State Taxation Administration revealed that since last year, they have been reminding taxpayers to conduct self-examinations of income obtained from overseas between 2022 and 2024. This means that many Chinese tax residents who hold overseas income accounts through activities such as speculating in US stocks, investing in offshore funds, or establishing offshore trusts may face retroactive tax audits for undeclared overseas income from previous years, and will be required to pay back taxes and late payment penalties. This retroactive tax audit occurred against the backdrop of China's implementation of the CRS system and a series of tax administration actions targeting overseas income (as shown in the table below). From the objective logic of tax supervision, the prerequisite for accurate audits by tax authorities is access to tax-related information. China first conducted CRS information exchange in 2018, exchanging account information for 2017. Based on this, Chinese tax authorities were able to obtain account balances, transaction records, and holder information of Chinese taxpayers in overseas banks, securities firms, trusts, and other institutions under the CRS exchange framework, making it possible to retroactively audit overseas income as far back as 2017.

Table 1: Review of Important Events in the Supervision of Foreign Income Tax

1.2 Observation of Regulatory Trends

A review of a series of enforcement actions by tax authorities over the past 25 years reveals that the issue of failure to declare foreign income in accordance with the law has become a key focus of supervision.

data.title: Aave v4 and capital efficiency
data.description:
Written by: Vaidik Mandloi & Translation: Block unicorn

Foreword

DeFi lending appears massive at first glance, but its underlying operation is inefficient. The same assets are scattered across multiple liquidity pools, markets, and chains. As a result, a large amount of capital is idle, not because of a lack of borrowers, but because of inadequate liquidity allocation.

This structure stems from the way DeFi protocols manage risk. Instead of charging different borrowers based on risk levels, protocols choose to create separate markets.

While this structure improves security, it also brings some unintended consequences. Even with the same underlying assets, each market requires its own liquidity. ETH deposited into the Aave core market cannot be used to fund loans in other independent markets, even if those markets have higher demand.

Over time, protocols eventually resulted in the same assets being scattered across multiple liquidity pools, each only partially utilized. Aave v4 aims to address this structural inefficiency. It centralizes liquidity and moves risk management to the edge of the system. Different types of lending still exist, but they no longer require separate liquidity pools. The idea is simple: utilize existing liquidity more efficiently while controlling risk. Why DeFi Lending Becomes Inefficient DeFi lending protocols manage risk by segmenting users into different markets rather than charging different prices. When a protocol wants to support assets with different risk levels, instead of fine-tuning interest rates or collateral costs, it creates separate markets. Assets deemed safe are grouped into one market, while newer or riskier assets are placed in a separate market with lower limits. High-leverage strategies are pushed into special modes, such as E-mode. The E model is designed for assets whose price movements are closely correlated, such as stablecoins or highly liquid collateralized tokens. Because these assets are highly correlated, the protocol allows users to lend and borrow more aggressively. Each market has its own set of rules, and more importantly, its own liquidity. This makes the system easier to control. If a market experiences a problem, the losses are limited to that market. However, this also means that even with the same underlying asset, liquidity cannot be shared across different markets. For example, ETH offered in the Aave core market can only be borrowed by users within that market. If lending demand increases in a separate market, the protocol cannot automatically transfer idle ETH from the core market to meet the demand. Liquidity can only be redistributed manually by users transferring funds. As Aave expands to various assets and blockchains, this pattern repeats itself. Each new risk category requires a new market, and each market requires its own liquidity. This results in assets being scattered across multiple liquidity pools, with no single pool being fully utilized. Despite the growth in total deposits, the protocol's ability to efficiently deploy capital has failed to keep pace. This structure also impacts pricing. Because borrowers are segmented by market rather than charged different fees based on risk level, users within the same market tend to pay similar interest rates, regardless of the actual safety of their collateral. Risk is present, but it's reflected through access restrictions rather than prices. Safer positions indirectly subsidize riskier positions, not by design, but due to restrictions. This creates a large and rigid system. Funds exist, but are constrained by market barriers. Supporting more assets or strategies requires more pools of funds, higher liquidity, and more diversified funding flows. This is precisely the problem Aave v4 aims to address. Separating Liquidity and Risk The core concept of Aave v4 is simple, but it requires a radical overhaul of how DeFi lending has always been structured. Liquidity and risk don't have to coexist. In previous versions, the market simultaneously performed two tasks: maintaining liquidity and enforcing risk rules. Because these two functions were closely intertwined, the only way to change risk was to split liquidity. This is precisely what caused market fragmentation. Aave v4 attempts to break this coupling. In version v4, liquidity on each chain is centralized in a place called the "Liquidity Hub." This hub is not a user-facing marketplace; it doesn't determine who can borrow, the amount borrowed, or the collateral. Its role is to safeguard assets, track supply and loan balances, calculate interest, and ensure the overall solvency of the system. All user interactions take place elsewhere. Next, Aave v4 abandons the concept of a "marketplace" and introduces Spokes (branch nodes). Spokes are not liquidity pools, but rather a set of rules that determine who can obtain liquidity under what conditions and with what risk limits. When users borrow, they do not borrow directly from Spokes, but rather from the Hub through Spokes.

Because Spokes do not hold liquidity, Aave v4 no longer needs to create new pools of funds every time it needs to support different types of risk. All assets are consolidated into a single balance sheet. The change between Spokes is not in where the funds are held, but in the rules for how the funds are used.

In earlier versions of Aave, these two were inseparable. The market defined both the risk assumptions and the pools of funds that supported those assumptions. If the protocol required stricter rules, higher leverage, or different collateral handling, a separate market had to be created with its own deposits. Over time, this led to the fragmentation of liquidity. The only way to change the risk was to split the capital.

... Aave v4 eliminates this limitation by separating accounting from access permissions. The Hub exists solely to hold assets, track positions, and ensure system-wide solvency. It does not determine which assets can be used as collateral, the allowed leverage ratios, or which oracles to trust. These decisions are handled by Spoke. Spoke is not a market in the traditional sense; it does not contain liquidity itself but defines a lending environment. When users borrow through Spoke, they borrow from the same underlying asset pool as other users, but are subject to specific rules. These rules determine how collateral is valued, the maximum amount that can be borrowed, and how positions are quickly liquidated when they become insecure. This structure allows the protocol to express drastically different risk appetites without duplicating funds. One branch can simulate today's conservative core markets. Another branch can set strict caps and parameters for higher-risk assets or allow higher leverage on related assets, similar to the E-Mode model. All these configurations can coexist, but none require pre-allocation to a separate liquidity pool. The key to avoiding dangerous situations is that Spoke does not allow unlimited access to liquidity. Each Spoke has clearly defined limits set by a governance mechanism. These limits define the risk exposure a Spoke can create and the types of assets it can interact with. If a Spoke begins to accumulate risk beyond expectations, these limits can be lowered. If the risk becomes completely unacceptable, the Spoke can be completely disabled without affecting the rest of the system or forcing other users to transfer funds. This is where capital efficiency truly improves. Liquidity is no longer reserved to cater to specific types of borrowers or strategies, but is available for use throughout the system and allocated through a set of rules rather than separate liquidity pools. The protocol can support more assets and strategies without constantly breaking down the balance sheet into smaller, less efficient parts. How Aave v4 Price Risk Liquidity sharing can only truly function when the protocol can differentiate between safe and insecure participants. In earlier versions, this differentiation was primarily structural. If you wanted safer lending, you entered a safer market. If you wanted higher leverage or used riskier collateral, you were allocated to another pool. Price differentiation existed, but it was simplistic and only implemented at the market level. Aave v4 moves risk differentiation from the market level to the borrower level. In version v4, asset interest rates are still based on a benchmark rate determined by supply and demand at the central node. What changes is the additional rate on top of the benchmark rate. Borrowers are no longer considered interchangeable simply because the borrowing assets are the same. Borrowing costs now depend on the collateral used for the loan. When a user borrows or lends through a Spoke, the protocol assesses the risk of the position based on the collateral, leverage ratio, and Spoke's rules. If the position is deemed high-risk, an additional fee, called a risk premium, is charged. Lower-risk positions pay a risk premium close to the benchmark interest rate, while higher-risk positions pay a higher risk premium. Spokes play a subtle but crucial role here. They not only control access to liquidity but also define how the risk premium is calculated and applied. Conservative Spokes may charge little or no premium because their allowed positions are already strictly limited. Spokes that allow higher-risk collateral or higher leverage ratios charge higher premiums to compensate for the system's risk exposure. These premiums flow back into the shared liquidity pool. Therefore, liquidity providers are compensated not only for liquidity usage but also for the level of risk the system undertakes. Capital is rewarded for supporting higher-risk activities, provided those risks are clearly priced in. Over time, this creates a feedback loop: if a certain type of lending is too risky, its cost increases; if demand shifts to safer allocations, prices adjust accordingly. The protocol doesn't require creating new markets to reflect this difference; it's directly reflected in interest rates. The resulting system more closely resembles a credit system where borrowers' value depends on their behavior and collateral, not just the market they access. Liquidity is shared, but risk is no longer averaged out. Unified Ledger and Clearing Once liquidity is shared, the most obvious concern is failure mode. What happens if everyone borrows from the same balance sheet and something goes wrong? In early designs, decentralization provided a crude form of protection. Losses were controlled because markets were isolated from each other. However, the concern with unified liquidity is that risk could spread further. Aave v4 addresses this by changing the protocol's ledger position and mandatory solvency. In version v3, each market actually maintained its own ledger. Solvency was assessed locally. Clearing was triggered within the pool, and losses were absorbed by the pool's liquidity. This made the market easier to understand, but it also meant the protocol lacked a global perspective on the cumulative risk across the entire system. In version 4, accounting has been moved to the central node (Hub). The central node maintains a unified view of accumulated assets, liabilities, and interest across the entire protocol. Every loan, regardless of its origin (Spoke), is recorded on the same balance sheet. This allows the protocol to assess solvency from a global perspective rather than market-by-market. It always has a grasp of total liquidity, outstanding debt, and remaining buffer funds. When a user opens a position through a Spoke, that position is still subject to the global solvency rules enforced by the central node. If a position becomes unsafe, liquidation logic is triggered according to the rules defined by the branch node, but settlement still uses the same underlying liquidity. Branch nodes define the timing and method of liquidation. The central node ensures that liquidation restores the system's solvency. Each Spoke has explicit risk exposure limits. These limits are designed to control the risks that each branch node may bring to the system. Even if all positions in a branch node fail simultaneously, the maximum loss is limited. Losses will not exceed the acceptable range set by the governance layer for that branch node. Other branch nodes can continue to operate normally because their liquidity access is unaffected. This uses a different failure model than the previous design. Version 4 isolates losses not by isolating liquidity, but by limiting risk exposure. The liquidation process also becomes more predictable. Due to unified accounting, liquidators only need to interface with a single source of liquidity. There is no need to transfer assets between markets or rebalance the liquidity pool during liquidation. This system doesn't rely on users transferring funds when the market is under pressure; instead, it relies on pre-set limits and consistent accounting. This reduces the likelihood of a cascading liquidation triggered by a liquidity shortage in a specific pool. In a fragmented design, a pool's collapse might not be due to a lack of funds within the system, but rather because funds flowed elsewhere. In version v4, liquidity shortages are a global signal. A unified accounting mechanism makes risk clear and manageable. The protocol always clearly understands where losses might occur, how large they might be, and which part of the system is responsible. It is this clarity that allows liquidity to be shared, preventing stress events from escalating into protocol-wide collapses. Long-term unlocking. Aave v4 is more than just a cleaner lending system. It also changes the speed and security of introducing new forms of risk in DeFi. In earlier versions, supporting new things always meant taking on structural risks. Whether listing new assets, experimenting with new collateral types, or accepting specific borrower groups, a new market with its own liquidity needs to be established. Governance decisions are also difficult because they impact capital allocation, and each attempt incurs the cost of market fragmentation. In version 4, experimentation becomes easier because liquidity no longer needs to be transferred. New branch nodes can be introduced without users depositing funds into the new liquidity pool. Governance mechanisms can define rules, limits, and pricing for specific use cases while maintaining balance sheet integrity. If the experiment succeeds, limits can be increased. If it fails, the branch node can be limited or closed without affecting other parts of the system. Instead of arguing whether an asset "deserves" a full market, new assets and strategies are viewed as limited risk exposures. This shifts the question from "Should we create a market?" to "How much risk are we willing to allocate?" This is a more precise decision and can be adjusted incrementally. This is particularly important for real-world asset-backed securities (RWAs) and institutional applications. RWAs often have limitations that make them difficult to fully integrate into existing markets, such as permissioning mechanisms, legal jargon, slower liquidation processes, or non-standard collateral behavior. In previous designs, accommodating these differences either required sacrificing the core market or completely isolating liquidity. In version 4, these limitations can exist within a spoke, with strict restrictions and custom rules, while still utilizing shared liquidity. In earlier versions, changing risk assumptions often required migrating markets or coordinating liquidity adjustments. In version 4, governance operates at the limit and rule level. Adjustments can be made incrementally, and risks can be gradually increased or decreased without forcing users to take action. This reduces governance costs and the cost of mistakes. Over time, this will lead to different growth patterns for the Aave protocol. Aave no longer scales by launching more markets and attracting isolated liquidity, but rather by increasing the use of its balance sheet. Capital efficiency is improved because liquidity no longer needs to be pre-allocated to specific markets and idle when demand shifts to other markets. This ultimately results in a DeFi protocol that more closely resembles the financial system.

data.title: Automated DeFi Platform Makina Finance Hit by Flash Loan Exploit, 1,299 ETH Taken from Curve Pool
data.description:
Makina Finance $4.13 Million Drain Hits Stablecoin Pool

The institutional-grade DeFi execution engine, Makina Finance, became the latest victim of a sophisticated on-chain heist early Tuesday morning.

What was meant to be a secure environment for high-level automated finance was turned into an open vault as a hacker managed to siphon 1,299 Ether from the protocol’s DUSD/USDC pool on Curve.

The breach, which unfolded with clinical precision, highlights a persistent vulnerability in how decentralized platforms interpret external data.

Security firm PeckShield was among the first to flag the suspicious movement, noting that the attacker targeted non-custodial liquidity providers by manipulating the very information the system relies on for pricing.

#PeckShieldAlert@makinafi has been exploited for ~1,299 $ETH (~$4.13M).

The hacker was frontrun by MEV Builder (0xa6c2...).

The stolen funds are currently held in 2 addresses:
0xbed2...dE25 ($3.3M) & 0x573d...910e ($880K) pic.twitter.com/Q5WzHpfq7j

— PeckShieldAlert (@PeckShieldAlert) January 20, 2026

How Did Flash Loans Break The Oracle

The attacker’s strategy relied on a massive temporary loan to create an illusion of value.

According to a security engineer at CertiK, the process began when the perpetrator took out a flash loan of 280 million USDC.

This capital was not used to trade, but rather to interfere with the MachineShareOracle, the component responsible for telling the Curve pool what its shares were worth.

By injecting 170 million USDC into specific points of the protocol mid-transaction, the hacker skewed the internal pricing data.

The CertiK engineer explained,

“A share-price oracle was pushed mid-tx, letting a Curve pool pay out at an inflated rate. ~5.1M USDC left the DUSD/USDC pool, the attacker profits about 4.1M.”

🚨 Another exploit today (4.1M):

Flashloan + permissionless AUM refresh is a dangerous combo.

A share-price oracle was pushed mid-tx, letting a Curve pool pay out at an inflated rate. ~5.1M USDC left the DUSD/USDC pool, the attacker profits about 4.1M. pic.twitter.com/t4RKYoUWDl

— n0b0dy (@nn0b0dyyy) January 20, 2026

Essentially, the protocol was tricked into believing its assets were far more valuable than they actually were, allowing the hacker to swap 110 million USDC against a pool that only held about $5 million in real liquidity.

Did An MEV Builder Snatch The Profits

In a strange twist of on-chain irony, the hacker did not walk away with the full amount they initially drained.

As the exploit was being executed, an automated Maximal Extractable Value (MEV) builder identified the opportunity and frontran the transaction.

This MEV entity, using an address starting with 0xa6c2, effectively "stole from the thief," capturing a significant portion of the value as the exploit played out.

CertiK estimates that this builder seized approximately $4.14 million of the $5 million withdrawn.

This sophisticated interception meant that while the pool was emptied, the original attacker had to settle for a split of the remaining assets.

The stolen funds were eventually converted into 1,299 ETH and distributed between two primary digital wallets.

Where Is The Stolen Ethereum Now

Blockchain trackers are currently focused on two specific addresses holding the spoils of the attack.

One wallet, 0xbed2…dE25, is holding roughly $3.3 million in ETH, while a second, 0xE573…f905, contains 276 ETH valued at approximately $880,000.

Unfortunately @makinafi got hacked for 1,299 $ETH or 4.13M dollars

A MEV was able to fron-run the hacker and the funds are still in these addresses custody:
0xbed2...dE25 ($3.3M)
0x573d...910e ($880K)

Makina strongly advises LPs in the DUSD Curve pool to remove their…

— Phantom Security (@PhantomOpSec) January 20, 2026

So far, the funds have remained stationary, avoiding the usual path toward mixing services or centralized exchanges where they could be frozen.

While the 1,299 ETH remains under a microscope, Makina Finance issued a brief statement at 6:42 AM UTC Tuesday, clarifying that the infrastructure of the entire protocol was not compromised.

On X, Makina Finance has urged liquidity providers in the DUSD pool to withdraw their remaining funds immediately while they determine “the appropriate next steps for affected users and LPs.”

Gmak, early this morning we received reports regarding an incident with the $DUSD Curve pool

At this stage, the issue appears to be isolated to DUSD LP positions on Curve. There is currently no indication that other assets or deployments are affected.

Underlying assets held in…

— Makina (@makinafi) January 20, 2026

Can Defi Recover From $16 Billion In Fraud

This incident adds to a grim tally for the broader crypto market, which is still reeling from a massive wave of illicit activity throughout 2025.

Data from the Cyvers Web3 Security and Fraud Report reveals that last year saw over $16 billion swindled through 108 distinct security incidents.

With nearly 19,000 active fraud networks identified globally, the Makina Finance exploit serves as a cold reality check for the industry.

Despite Makina’s status as an institutional-grade platform with over $100 million in total value locked, the breach proves that even the most well-capitalized protocols are vulnerable to the rapid-fire logic of flash loan attacks and oracle manipulation.

data.title: The Rise of Perp DEX: A Structural Revolution in On-Chain Derivatives
data.description:
One ​​of the most significant changes in the crypto market over the past two years has not been a new public blockchain or a popular narrative, but rather the slow but steady migration of derivatives trading from centralized exchanges to on-chain transactions. In this process, Perpetual DEXs (decentralized exchanges with perpetual contracts) have gradually grown from an experimental product into one of the most lucrative sectors within the DeFi ecosystem.

If spot trading is the starting point of DeFi, then perpetual contracts are becoming its true "cash flow core."

Why is Perp DEX rising?

In the traditional crypto trading system, perpetual contracts have long been the most important source of profit for centralized exchanges. Whether it's transaction fees, funding rates, or the additional revenue from liquidation, pyramid schemes almost monopolize the entire derivatives cash flow.

For DeFi, this isn't a question of "whether or not to do it," but rather "whether or not they have the capability to do it." Early DeFi lacked the fundamental conditions to support perpetual contracts. Insufficient on-chain performance led to high transaction latency, expensive gas costs, and infrequent price oracle updates, making any leveraged product vulnerable to arbitrageurs. Even with attempts, it was difficult to compete with centralized exchanges (CEXs) in terms of user experience and risk control. The real turning point came after the infrastructure matured. The widespread adoption of Layer 2 and the emergence of high-performance public chains significantly improved on-chain transaction throughput and latency; next-generation oracle systems provided faster and more stable price data; and DeFi users, having weathered multiple cycles, were no longer just "mining users" but gradually evolved into market participants with professional trading capabilities. More importantly, the trust crisis of centralized exchanges became the final straw that broke the camel's back. Asset freezes, misappropriation risks, and regulatory uncertainty have led more and more high-frequency traders and large funds to re-examine the cost of "custody." Against this backdrop, Perp DEX offers a new possibility: regaining control of asset ownership without sacrificing leverage and liquidity. Essentially, the rise of Perp DEX represents a redistribution of derivatives profits from centralized institutions to on-chain users. Why Perpetual Contracts are the Most Suitable DeFi Derivative Form: Among all derivatives, perpetual contracts are almost tailor-made for DeFi. Compared to futures contracts, perpetual contracts have no expiration date and do not require frequent rollovers; compared to options, they have a simpler structure and more intuitive pricing, allowing users to focus on direction and leverage without needing to understand complex Greeks or volatility models. More importantly, perpetual contracts offer extremely high trading frequency. They are not "event-driven" products, but rather infrastructure that continuously generates trading demand. This is crucial for any protocol that relies on transaction fees and liquidity. For this reason, almost all successful Perp DEXs design their products around the same goal: to maximize trading frequency while minimizing transaction costs. Whether by reducing slippage, decreasing latency, or optimizing clearing efficiency, the ultimate aim is to attract more professional traders to remain on-chain long-term. What problems does Perp DEX truly solve? Many people simply understand Perp DEX as a "decentralized version of a centralized exchange," but this underestimates its significance. Perp DEX is not replicating centralized exchanges, but rather reconstructing the underlying logic of derivatives trading. First, it changes the trust model. In Perp DEX, user funds are always held in custody by smart contracts, and the protocol itself cannot arbitrarily misappropriate assets. Risk exposure, margin, and liquidation logic are all publicly verifiable, meaning traders no longer need to "trust" the platform's risk control but can directly audit the rules themselves. Second, it makes risk pricing more transparent. Centralized exchanges' liquidation, mark-price, and funding rates are essentially black-box mechanisms. On-chain, however, these parameters are explicitly defined by contracts, allowing anyone to see how the market is liquidated and rebalanced. Finally, there's the change in profit distribution. Perp DEX doesn't concentrate all trading profits at the platform level; instead, it returns the cash flow generated by derivatives to on-chain participants through LPs, Vaults, and governance tokens. This makes users both traders and potential "shareholders" of the protocol. From this perspective, Perp DEX is more like an on-chain risk management system than just a trading front-end. How does the core mechanism of Perp DEX work? From a mechanism perspective, the evolution of Perp DEX has undergone a clear professionalization process. Early protocols mostly adopted the vAMM model, using virtual liquidity pools to solve the liquidity cold start problem. However, this approach is prone to slippage under large transactions and is highly dependent on arbitrageurs for correction. As trading volume increased, the order book model was gradually introduced. On-chain or semi-on-chain orderbooks allow market makers to directly place orders, significantly improving depth and price discovery capabilities. In reality, most protocols choose a compromise: off-chain matching and on-chain settlement, or combining AMM with limit orders to balance decentralization and trading performance. Behind these models, the real risk lies with the liquidity providers. LPs are essentially betting against all traders, earning fees and funding rates while bearing the risk of market direction. If the protocol's risk control is poorly designed, the long-term profits of professional traders will ultimately translate into systemic losses for LPs. Therefore, mature Perp DEXs invest heavily in liquidation mechanisms, insurance funds, and parameter adjustments. Liquidation is not a punishment, but a necessary means to maintain system stability. Whoever can complete liquidation quickly and accurately under extreme market conditions is qualified for long-term survival. Where exactly does Perp DEX's competitive advantage lie? To determine if a Perp DEX has long-term value, one cannot simply look at the interface or incentives; rather, one must examine whether it has established a genuine competitive advantage. Liquidity depth is the first hurdle; without stable liquidity, even the best mechanisms cannot attract large funds. The security of the clearing system and oracles is the second hurdle; any serious delay or error will directly shake market confidence. The third hurdle is the ability to retain professional traders and market makers, which depends on latency, fees, and the overall trading experience. Ultimately, all competitive advantages point to the same question: can the protocol be profitable in the long term without relying on subsidies? Only by generating positive cash flow can Perp DEX become true infrastructure, rather than a short-term narrative. How to use data to determine if a Perp DEX is healthy? At the investment research level, Perp DEX has a relatively clear evaluation framework. The relationship between trading volume and TVL reflects capital utilization, and the comparison between overall trader profits and losses and LP returns reveals whether risk control is reasonable. Stable funding rates and frequent, dispersed liquidations are often more important than daily trading volume. Furthermore, the number of active traders and the protocol revenue structure can determine whether the platform has truly established user stickiness, rather than relying on short-term incentives to accumulate data. The Most Easily Overlooked Risks in Perp DEX Many risks do not stem from leverage itself, but from system details. Oracle delays can be amplified in extreme market conditions, liquidity can dry up instantly during periods of high volatility, and untimely adjustments to governance parameters can trigger a chain reaction. These risks do not occur every day, but when they do, they are often fatal. Understanding these "low-frequency, high-impact" risks is a prerequisite for using Perp DEX. Case Study: Hyperliquid's "Extreme Attempt at Specialization" with On-Chain Perpetual Contracts While most Perp DEXs start with "how to replicate the CEX experience in a DeFi environment," Hyperliquid's approach has been different from the beginning. It doesn't simply "build a Perp" on an existing public chain; instead, it redesigns an entire underlying infrastructure for the highly specialized scenario of perpetual contract trading. Hyperliquid's choice to develop its own high-performance L1/Appchain is essentially a very radical but logically sound trade-off: sacrificing generality for specialization in exchange for matching efficiency, latency, and risk control certainty. This also determines that its target users are not general DeFi users, but rather high-frequency traders who are extremely sensitive to execution quality, slippage, and capital efficiency. In terms of trading mechanisms, Hyperliquid employs a fully on-chain Orderbook, rather than a vAMM or semi-off-chain matching system. This is crucial. An Orderbook means the price discovery process is closer to that of a traditional derivatives exchange, but it also significantly increases the demands on system performance, liquidation engines, and risk control models. Hyperliquid places liquidation and risk control at the system level, rather than as a post-event remedy, making its behavior more predictable under extreme market conditions. From an on-chain data perspective, the most valuable aspect of Hyperliquid is not a single metric, but rather the "combination relationships" between metrics. On DefiLlama, you can observe that Hyperliquid has consistently maintained a very high daily trading volume/TVL ratio. This isn't just a result of "volume manipulation," but a clear signal: the liquidity entering the system is being used frequently and intensively, rather than lying idle in pools waiting for subsidies. High capital efficiency often signifies high-quality traders. Further analysis of the active trader structure on Dune reveals that Hyperliquid's daily and weekly active users don't experience brief spikes during airdrops or events, but rather exhibit a relatively smooth and consistent pattern. This type of curve typically corresponds to "tool-type usage" rather than "mining-type participation." For investment research, this is a crucial dividing line.

By combining Nansen analysis with observations of large account behavior, it becomes easier to understand Hyperliquid's true competitive advantage: the system has consistently participating professional accounts whose trading behavior exhibits consistent strategies, rather than one-off games. This means that what is happening at Hyperliquid is not "attracting users to try it out," but rather traders migrating to their primary trading venue.

data.title: Robotics × Crypto: The Next Big Crypto Narrative
data.description:
Author: Diego, Crypto Analyst; Translated by: Jinse Finance

>

Initially, I was skeptical.

Then I did some research (over 20 hours of study) and realized that robotics will be the biggest economic opportunity of our time.

This industry combines the most powerful technologies we have today: artificial intelligence, machines, and cryptocurrencies, which is why investors are pouring billions of dollars into it.

Robots (whether humanoid or not) will soon be a part of our daily lives, but people haven't realized it yet.

Meanwhile, Nvidia is telling us that the next wave of hype won't revolve around LLM, but around physical AI, which is to say, around robotics.

In 2026, the market will see phenomenal growth; all the conditions are in place, and cryptocurrency-related projects will take center stage.

In this article, we'll explore the development trends of robotics and identify the top cryptocurrency projects in the field.

If you want to know how to get my table (which tracks all the major robotics projects in the cryptocurrency space), please read to the end.

2026 is the Year of Robotics

CES is the world's most important technology exhibition, and guess what? Robotics is the theme of this year's show.

Most companies are from China, but the US is closing the gap.

In short, it's worth noting that, depending on the situation in different countries, robotics is finally going mainstream and will become a core part of our daily lives much faster than we imagine.

It's still early, but I think the time left is running out.

2026 will be the Year of the Robot, for Three Reasons

More interestingly, it's about understanding why now is the time for robots to finally go mainstream.

Understanding this also helps explain why the robotics narrative in the cryptocurrency space has gained rapid attention on CT.

@KaitoAI: Attention to robotics on CT is on the rise. But on average, it's still 6 times lower than that for artificial intelligence.

LLM has finally evolved into sufficiently intelligent artificial intelligence, acting as the brain of physical machines, now capable of performing real actions and "controlling" robots. ChatGPT 3 is a turning point.

Hardware is becoming increasingly accessible.

Production costs are dropping dramatically. Humanoid robots that cost over $100,000 a few years ago will soon cost around $20,000.

Hardware is becoming increasingly accessible.

Production costs are dropping dramatically. Humanoid robots that cost over $100,000 a few years ago will soon cost around $20,000.

... Top robotics companies such as Tesla, Figure AI, and Boston Dynamics have announced the launch of more affordable humanoid robots. Governments around the world are investing billions of dollars in robotics. The United States and China are engaged in fierce competition, and both countries understand that falling behind in this race would pose a strategic threat.

Cryptocurrency × Robots: A Perfect Combination

What most people don't realize is that robots will be using cryptographic tracks in their daily operations.

That's why cryptocurrency and robotics are poised to be one of the hottest sectors this year.

Robots will naturally prefer to use cryptographic infrastructure rather than centralized, opaque systems. That's undeniable.

Why?

Because today's financial and digital infrastructure was built for humans, not robots. For example?

When opening a bank account, you need to provide the following information: name, address, place of residence, and job.

If that's all there is to it, then humanoid robots would never be able to open a bank account.

Blockchain simplifies this process. In our example, humanoid robots will be able to use Web3 wallets, which don't require any of the above data and allow them to spend money freely and quickly.

For robots, using a Web3 wallet is far more logical than opening an account at JPMorgan Chase.

Top Projects in the Robot Narrative

It's time to reveal the top 7 cryptocurrency projects in the robotics field.

This isn't a list of all projects, but rather a list of what I believe are currently the best-performing projects.

1. PEAQ — Robot Blockchain

PEAQ is a Layer-1 blockchain positioned as a coordinating layer for the machine economy. In fact, Peaq enables the creation of DePIN applications built on its blockchain (e.g., Silencio Network, which will be discussed in detail later). Users can interact with applications built on Peaq and earn money through devices, bots, and machines that generate real economic value. The PEAQ token launched in November 2024 and currently has a market capitalization of approximately $55 million. It serves as an on-chain fuel token and an incentive mechanism for the ecosystem. Peaq: The first tokenized bot has just paid out to its token holders. → Tokenized bot farm grows fresh vegetables → Ordinary people will buy them → A portion of the profits will be distributed to token holders on Peaq. This is not just a matter of converting Web2 liquidity into Web3 rewards. This is Peaq's vision—robots working. [Image of Peaq with images: https://img.jinse.com.cn/7427279_watermarknone.png] [Image of Peaq with images: https://x.com/peaq/status/2005960139841356047/photo/1] [Image of XMAQUINA—an investment DAO in the robotics field] [XMAQUINA is a DAO that allows anyone to invest in early-stage robotics companies that are not yet publicly listed on a stock exchange.] Most robotics companies are still privately held, meaning that even if you are bullish on the robotics industry, you usually cannot invest in them. With its funding (currently around $35 million), the XMAQUINA community can participate in private transactions in the robotics market. The proceeds from these investments will be distributed to DAO members. Its token, DEUS, represents the DAO's distribution mechanism. To date, the DAO has invested in companies such as Figure, Apptronik, Agility Robotics, NEURA Robotics, and 1X. 3. GEODNET — The Data Infrastructure for the Robotics Revolution GEODNET is building an RTK network, which will be invaluable for future robots. The RTK (Real-Time Kinematic) network provides centimeter-level positioning accuracy. GEODNET is building this infrastructure using physical base stations and a decentralized network designed around the DePIN principle. Users who install these base stations will receive GEOD rewards. This incentive mechanism works well, enabling GEODNET to build the world's largest RTK network, usable by robots, drones, autonomous vehicles, and more. 4. AUKI — Building a Real-World Network Auki's mission is to enable artificial intelligence to access the physical world by building a "real-world network" for robots and intelligent machines. In essence, AUKI is building a network designed to enable digital devices to exchange spatial data and computing power. This is crucial for robotics, as it allows robots to move in our world, understand it, and interact with it effectively. Using these services requires payment, while providing resources is rewarded. In short, machines pay each other to collectively understand the real world. The token AUKI incentivizes contributors and serves as a value accumulation mechanism as users pay to access the services offered by the network. 5. PrismaX — Massive Remote Operations PrismaX is building an open coordination layer connecting remote operators, robot users, fleet owners, and robotics companies. In fact, PrismaX allows ordinary users like us to remotely operate autonomous robots around the world and earn money from this activity. Operators can connect with users, remotely operate robots to complete real-world tasks, and generate highly valuable data in the process. Robotics companies can connect with fleet owners interested in deploying robot fleets specifically for data collection. The token has not yet been officially issued, but a points reward program for early users is underway. An airdrop is expected soon. 6. CodecFlow – Robotics Execution Engine CodecFlow is a platform that provides a fully isolated, on-demand computing environment for building and testing robots. Imagine you want to build your own humanoid robot. Where would you start? What kind of environment would you use to safely test, train, and improve it? CodecFlow was created for this purpose; it's an all-in-one platform that helps robotics teams create, test, and iterate their products in a secure and reliable digital environment. This product is currently being tested in multiple robotics teams. Once fully launched, protocol fees will be used to buy back CODEC tokens. The token, launched in May 2025, is already listed on Solana and currently has a market capitalization of approximately $7 million. 7. Silencio — The Ear of the Global AI and Robotics Industry Built on the PeaQ blockchain, Silencio Network focuses on collecting real-world audio and voice data. AI systems and robots need high-quality audio data to understand and interact with the physical world, and Silencio is building the infrastructure to provide this data. To date, Silencio has distributed over $3 million in rewards to participants who collect and share audio data. Getting started is simple. Users simply download the Silencio app on their smartphones, collect audio data, and earn rewards for their contributions. The SLC token, launched in February 2025, currently has a market capitalization of approximately $3 million. This token is used as an incentive mechanism for participants.

data.title: Unveiling the X Traffic Secrets: A Practical Guide to Creating Viral Articles
data.description:
Author: Jesse Source: X, @jessezheng

Jason has already explained the X recommendation mechanism very clearly! I'll supplement it with a structured, practical guide; this is the traffic code hidden in the source code.

1. Weight Ranking: Sharing is the Hard Currency

The algorithm scores different behaviors completely differently: Sharing (RT) > Reply > Like.

Dwell time is very important: If your text can make people stop and read it to the end, the algorithm will determine that it is high-quality content and continue to push it (please read slowly, dear readers).

2. Visual Enhancement: The Era of Pure Text is Over

The source code shows that images and videos have special weight multipliers.

Tweets with images have a much higher exposure chance than plain text (nona banana, get it!).

Video content has a very high distribution priority in the current algorithm-driven recommendation stream (For You).

3. Don't drive traffic out (external links are a no-no)

The algorithm's KPI is user retention. Tweets with YouTube or external website links will trigger weight penalties (if you really need them, put them in the comments section).

4. Don't be a "general store"

X uses SimClusters logic to categorize you. If you post about technology today and pets tomorrow, the algorithm will stop pushing your feed because it can't target your audience.

So stay vertical.

The more focused you are on a particular field, the higher the "weighting" of that circle on you. 5. Blue Label Support Blue Label (Premium) is a "traffic plug-in" hard-coded in the source code, increasing your weight several times over. To earn creator revenue, you must pay first. What other methods can increase the influence of an X account? I think topicality is also very important. For example, my recent posts about Manus and housing in the UAE went viral because these are issues that people care about. Additionally, sincere sharing that resonates with others can also gain attention, such as my reflections on investment mistakes over the past year. To summarize a viral template: Start with a poignant hook + high-definition images/videos + vertical, informative content + interactive comments.
